1:
"Then Joshua called the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh," The witnesses note Joshua calling the eastern tribes, the war being ended and the land divided. The witnesses note that Gill notes the promise of God being fulfilled, the land subdued, the war at an end, rest had on all sides, and the land divided. The witnesses note that he called Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua called — the eastern tribes; the the war ended — the land subdued; the rest on all sides — the promise kept; the the labour completed — a rest remains for God's people (Heb. 4:9).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with the great work accomplished — "the promise of God being fulfilled, the land subdued, the war at an end": now Joshua calls the eastern tribes, who had crossed Jordan to fight, to release them home; the calling of these tribes after the conquest shows the faithfulness of God in giving rest, and the order of a work brought to its close; it teaches that God brings his people's labours to completion and grants them rest, the warfare ended and the promise kept, "there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God" (Heb. 4:9), Joshua calling the eastern tribes when the war was done.

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua called them: The witnesses note the Joshua called: the eastern tribes. The witnesses note the the war ended: the land subdued. The witnesses note the rest on all sides: the promise kept. The witnesses note the the labour completed: a rest remains for God's people (Heb. 4:9). The expositors observe the Joshua called: the eastern tribes. The expositors observe the the war ended: the land subdued. The expositors observe the rest on all sides: the promise kept. The expositors observe the the labour completed: a rest remains for God's people (Heb. 4:9). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua called, the the war ended, the rest on all sides, the the labour completed, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Joshua called the eastern tribes when the war was ended and the land divided — God bringing his people's labours to completion and granting them rest, a rest remaining for the people of God (Heb. 4:9).
2:
"And said unto them, Ye have kept all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you, and have obeyed my voice in all that I commanded you:" The witnesses note Joshua commending the eastern tribes for keeping Moses' command and obeying his voice. The witnesses note that Gill notes the command was that they should leave their families and flocks in Gilead and pass over Jordan to fight with their brethren. The witnesses note that they had obeyed all Joshua commanded. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye have kept — moses' command; the obeyed my voice — all I commanded; the the pledge made good — fighting for their brethren; the obedience commended — well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21).

The witnesses agree Joshua bears honourable witness to the faithfulness of the eastern tribes — "ye have kept all that Moses... commanded you, and have obeyed my voice": they had made good their pledge, leaving their families to fight for their brethren until the land was won; the commendation of their steadfast obedience shows that faithful service is owned and praised by godly leaders; it teaches that God and his servants take note of and commend the obedience of his people, the faithful word kept being a thing of honour, "well done, thou good and faithful servant" (Matt. 25:21), Joshua commending the eastern tribes' obedience.

So the witnesses make the verse ye have kept all: The witnesses note the ye have kept: Moses' command. The witnesses note the obeyed my voice: all I commanded. The witnesses note the the pledge made good: fighting for their brethren. The witnesses note the obedience commended: well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21). The expositors observe the ye have kept: Moses' command. The expositors observe the obeyed my voice: all I commanded. The expositors observe the the pledge made good: fighting for their brethren. The expositors observe the obedience commended: well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21). The believer is taught to mark the ye have kept, the obeyed my voice, the the pledge made good, the obedience commended, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Joshua commended the eastern tribes for keeping Moses' command and obeying his voice — faithful service owned and praised, the kept word a thing of honour, well done, good and faithful servant (Matt. 25:21).
3:
"Ye have not left your brethren these many days unto this day, but have kept the charge of the commandment of the LORD your God." The witnesses note the eastern tribes' perseverance — not leaving their brethren through many years of war. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was for fourteen years — seven in subduing the land, seven in dividing it. The witnesses note that they kept the charge of the commandment of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not left your brethren — many days; the through the long war — fourteen years; the kept the charge — of the commandment; the brotherly perseverance — bear one another's burdens (Gal. 6:2).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes' constancy through the long years is here praised — "ye have not left your brethren these many days... but have kept the charge of the commandment": for some fourteen years, Gill notes, they stood by their brethren, enduring the whole war though their own land was already won; the perseverance that held to the end, when self-interest might have called them home, marks a true and brotherly faithfulness; it teaches that the people of God are to persevere in their duty to one another to the end, not wearying in well-doing, "bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ" (Gal. 6:2), the eastern tribes not leaving their brethren many days.

So the witnesses make the verse not left your brethren: The witnesses note the not left your brethren: many days. The witnesses note the through the long war: fourteen years. The witnesses note the kept the charge: of the commandment. The witnesses note the brotherly perseverance: bear one another's burdens (Gal. 6:2). The expositors observe the not left your brethren: many days. The expositors observe the through the long war: fourteen years. The expositors observe the kept the charge: of the commandment. The expositors observe the brotherly perseverance: bear one another's burdens (Gal. 6:2). The believer is taught to mark the not left your brethren, the through the long war, the kept the charge, the brotherly perseverance, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes did not leave their brethren through the long war but kept the charge — true brotherly faithfulness persevering to the end, bearing one another's burdens, fulfilling the law of Christ (Gal. 6:2).
4:
"And now the LORD your God hath given rest unto your brethren, as he promised them: therefore now return ye, and get you unto your tents, and unto the land of your possession..." The witnesses note Joshua releasing the tribes to their homes, the LORD having given rest as promised. The witnesses note that Gill notes the rest given to their brethren as the Lord had promised. The witnesses note that Wesley notes, though longing for their families, they like good soldiers would not return till dismissed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD hath given rest — as promised; the return to your tents — now released; the like good soldiers — waiting till dismissed (Wesley); the the promise kept — he gave them rest round about (Josh. 21:44).

The witnesses agree the time of release came because "the LORD your God hath given rest unto your brethren, as he promised": the rest being secured, the eastern tribes might now go home; Wesley marks that, though they longed for their families, "like good soldiers they would not return till dismissed" — their discipline as commendable as their courage; it teaches that the servants of God are to await his time and their leaders' word, not running before they are sent, and that God faithfully gives the rest he promises, "he had given them rest round about" (Josh. 21:44), the tribes now released to their possession.

So the witnesses make the verse return to your tents: The witnesses note the the LORD hath given rest: as promised. The witnesses note the return to your tents: now released. The witnesses note the like good soldiers: waiting till dismissed (Wesley). The witnesses note the the promise kept: he gave them rest round about (Josh. 21:44). The expositors observe the the LORD hath given rest: as promised. The expositors observe the return to your tents: now released. The expositors observe the like good soldiers: waiting till dismissed (Wesley). The expositors observe the the promise kept: he gave them rest round about (Josh. 21:44). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD hath given rest, the return to your tents, the like good soldiers, the the promise kept, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Joshua released the tribes home, the LORD having given rest as promised — the disciplined awaiting their dismissal like good soldiers, God faithfully giving the rest he promised, rest round about (Josh. 21:44).
5:
"But take diligent heed to do the commandment and the law, which Moses the servant of the LORD charged you, to love the LORD your God, and to walk in all his ways, and to keep his commandments, and to cleave unto him, and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul." The witnesses note Joshua's charge — to love the LORD, walk in his ways, cleave to him, and serve him with all the heart. The witnesses note that Gill notes the commandment and the law — the ten commandments and all other laws, moral, ceremonial, and civil. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the charge to do the commandment Moses gave. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the love the LORD — the great commandment; the walk in all his ways — obedience; the cleave unto him — steadfast devotion; the serve with all your heart — love the LORD with all thine heart (Deut. 6:5).

The witnesses agree this verse gathers the whole of true religion into one rich charge — "to love the LORD... walk in all his ways... keep his commandments... cleave unto him... serve him with all your heart and with all your soul": love, obedience, cleaving, and wholehearted service are joined as the sum of all duty; the charge echoes the great commandment of the law, "thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart" (Deut. 6:5), which Christ named the first and great commandment (Matt. 22:37); it teaches that the substance of godliness is whole-hearted love and devotion to God, the prosperous and the resting no less bound to cleave to him, the eastern tribes charged to love and serve the LORD with all their heart.

So the witnesses make the verse cleave unto him: The witnesses note the love the LORD: the great commandment. The witnesses note the walk in all his ways: obedience. The witnesses note the cleave unto him: steadfast devotion. The witnesses note the serve with all your heart: love the LORD with all thine heart (Deut. 6:5). The expositors observe the love the LORD: the great commandment. The expositors observe the walk in all his ways: obedience. The expositors observe the cleave unto him: steadfast devotion. The expositors observe the serve with all your heart: love the LORD with all thine heart (Deut. 6:5). The believer is taught to mark the love the LORD, the walk in all his ways, the cleave unto him, the serve with all your heart, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Joshua charged the tribes to love, obey, cleave to, and serve the LORD with all their heart — the whole of true religion in one charge, the first and great commandment, to love the LORD with all thine heart and soul (Deut. 6:5; Matt. 22:37).
6:
"So Joshua blessed them, and sent them away: and they went unto their tents." The witnesses note Joshua blessing the eastern tribes and sending them home. The witnesses note that Gill notes he dismissed them with a blessing, wishing them well and praying God's blessing on them and their families, who had been so useful. The witnesses note that they went unto their tents. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua blessed them — with a prayer; the sent them away — home; the the faithful honoured — crowned with blessing; the the parting benediction — the LORD bless thee, and keep thee (Num. 6:24).

The witnesses agree Joshua sent the faithful tribes home not empty but "blessed... and sent them away": the parting benediction, Gill notes, was a prayer for God's blessing on them and their households; the leader's blessing crowned their faithful service with his good word and prayer; it teaches that the servants of God are to be sent on their way with blessing, and that those who serve faithfully are dismissed with honour and prayer, the good word of God's minister following them, "the LORD bless thee, and keep thee" (Num. 6:24), Joshua blessing the tribes and sending them home.

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua blessed them: The witnesses note the Joshua blessed them: with a prayer. The witnesses note the sent them away: home. The witnesses note the the faithful honoured: crowned with blessing. The witnesses note the the parting benediction: the LORD bless thee, and keep thee (Num. 6:24). The expositors observe the Joshua blessed them: with a prayer. The expositors observe the sent them away: home. The expositors observe the the faithful honoured: crowned with blessing. The expositors observe the the parting benediction: the LORD bless thee, and keep thee (Num. 6:24). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua blessed them, the sent them away, the the faithful honoured, the the parting benediction, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Joshua blessed the eastern tribes and sent them home — the faithful dismissed with honour and prayer, the leader's blessing crowning their service, the LORD bless thee and keep thee (Num. 6:24).
7:
"Now to the one half of the tribe of Manasseh Moses had given possession in Bashan: but unto the other half thereof gave Joshua among their brethren on this side Jordan westward..." The witnesses note the tribe of Manasseh divided, half settled east by Moses, half west by Joshua. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses had given the one half possession in Bashan, the kingdom of Og. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set half Manasseh east and half west of Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the half in Bashan — given by Moses; the half west — given by Joshua; the one tribe parted — yet one people; the one in faith — one body, one Lord, one faith (Eph. 4:4-5).

The witnesses agree the verse notes the dividing of Manasseh, half settled east of Jordan by Moses and half west by Joshua: the one tribe was parted between the two regions, yet remained one people under the one God; the division of a tribe between Jordan's sides, while keeping their unity, foreshadows how God's people may be scattered in place yet joined in faith; it teaches that the people of God, though dispersed in their dwellings, are one in him, no distance breaking the bond of the covenant, "one body, and one Spirit... one Lord, one faith" (Eph. 4:4-5), Manasseh divided yet one.

So the witnesses make the verse Manasseh divided: The witnesses note the half in Bashan: given by Moses. The witnesses note the half west: given by Joshua. The witnesses note the one tribe parted: yet one people. The witnesses note the one in faith: one body, one Lord, one faith (Eph. 4:4-5). The expositors observe the half in Bashan: given by Moses. The expositors observe the half west: given by Joshua. The expositors observe the one tribe parted: yet one people. The expositors observe the one in faith: one body, one Lord, one faith (Eph. 4:4-5). The believer is taught to mark the half in Bashan, the half west, the one tribe parted, the one in faith, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that Manasseh was divided, half east and half west of Jordan, yet one people — God's people dispersed in place yet joined in faith, no distance breaking the covenant bond, one body, one Lord, one faith (Eph. 4:4-5).
8:
"And he spake unto them, saying, Return with much riches unto your tents, and with very much cattle... and with very much raiment: divide the spoil of your enemies with your brethren." The witnesses note Joshua bidding them return rich with spoil, and divide it with their brethren. The witnesses note that Gill notes he spake to the half tribe of Manasseh, though some think to the other tribes also. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they returned with their full proportion of the spoils taken from the Canaanites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the return with much riches — the spoil; the cattle and raiment — their full share; the divide with your brethren — fair sharing; the all partaking — as his part that tarried (1 Sam. 30:24).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes went home laden with the spoils of the conquest — "return with much riches... divide the spoil... with your brethren": they had their full share of the plunder, and were charged to share it with the brethren who had kept their homes; the bidding to divide the spoil with those who stayed behind teaches the law of fair sharing, that they who tarried by the stuff should partake with they who went to war (1 Sam. 30:24); it teaches that the gains of God's people are to be shared, the blessing of the few made the portion of the many, "they should be partakers of his hope" (cf. 1 Cor. 9:10), the spoil divided with the brethren.

So the witnesses make the verse divide the spoil: The witnesses note the return with much riches: the spoil. The witnesses note the cattle and raiment: their full share. The witnesses note the divide with your brethren: fair sharing. The witnesses note the all partaking: as his part that tarried (1 Sam. 30:24). The expositors observe the return with much riches: the spoil. The expositors observe the cattle and raiment: their full share. The expositors observe the divide with your brethren: fair sharing. The expositors observe the all partaking: as his part that tarried (1 Sam. 30:24). The believer is taught to mark the return with much riches, the cattle and raiment, the divide with your brethren, the all partaking, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes returned rich and were charged to divide the spoil with their brethren — the law of fair sharing, the gains of God's people made the portion of all, as his part that tarried by the stuff (1 Sam. 30:24).
9:
"And the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh returned... to go unto the country of Gilead, to the land of their possession..." The witnesses note the eastern tribes setting out for Gilead, the land of their possession. The witnesses note that Gill notes they returned first to the camp to take their substance, then set forward to their own land. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them returning to Gilead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the eastern tribes returned — homeward; the to Gilead — their possession; the their service done — the labour fulfilled; the entering their rest — they rest from their labours (Rev. 14:13).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes now departed for "the country of Gilead, to the land of their possession": their long service done, they turned homeward to enjoy the inheritance Moses had given them; the return to their own land after faithful labour pictures the entering into rest that follows duty fulfilled; it teaches that those who have served God faithfully come at last to the enjoyment of their portion, the homeward journey after the warfare a figure of the believer's entrance into his rest, "they rest from their labours" (Rev. 14:13), the tribes returning to Gilead.

So the witnesses make the verse returned to Gilead: The witnesses note the the eastern tribes returned: homeward. The witnesses note the to Gilead: their possession. The witnesses note the their service done: the labour fulfilled. The witnesses note the entering their rest: they rest from their labours (Rev. 14:13). The expositors observe the the eastern tribes returned: homeward. The expositors observe the to Gilead: their possession. The expositors observe the their service done: the labour fulfilled. The expositors observe the entering their rest: they rest from their labours (Rev. 14:13). The believer is taught to mark the the eastern tribes returned, the to Gilead, the their service done, the entering their rest, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes returned to Gilead, the land of their possession — those who served faithfully coming to enjoy their portion, the homeward journey a figure of entering rest, they rest from their labours (Rev. 14:13).
10:
"And when they came unto the borders of Jordan, that are in the land of Canaan, the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh built there an altar by Jordan, a great altar to see to." The witnesses note the eastern tribes building a great altar by Jordan, on the Canaan side. The witnesses note that Gill notes they came to the banks of Jordan, and built the altar on the Canaan side. The witnesses note that it was a great altar to see to. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came to Jordan — before crossing; the built an altar — by Jordan; the a great altar to see to — conspicuous; the a memorial of faith — a sign when your children ask (Josh. 4:6).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes, before crossing, "built there an altar by Jordan, a great altar to see to": the building of so conspicuous an altar, on the Canaan side, was meant as a lasting and visible witness; though it would soon be gravely misunderstood, their purpose, as later appears, was to keep a memorial of their share in the LORD and his altar, lest in time to come the western tribes disown them; it teaches the value of visible memorials of faith and union among God's people, set up that the generations to come might remember their part in the covenant, "this shall be a sign... when your children ask" (cf. Josh. 4:6), the great altar built by Jordan.

So the witnesses make the verse a great altar: The witnesses note the came to Jordan: before crossing. The witnesses note the built an altar: by Jordan. The witnesses note the a great altar to see to: conspicuous. The witnesses note the a memorial of faith: a sign when your children ask (Josh. 4:6). The expositors observe the came to Jordan: before crossing. The expositors observe the built an altar: by Jordan. The expositors observe the a great altar to see to: conspicuous. The expositors observe the a memorial of faith: a sign when your children ask (Josh. 4:6). The believer is taught to mark the came to Jordan, the built an altar, the a great altar to see to, the a memorial of faith, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes built a great altar by Jordan — a conspicuous and lasting witness, a memorial of their part in the LORD and his altar, that the generations might remember, a sign when your children ask (Josh. 4:6).
11:
"And the children of Israel heard say, Behold, the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh have built an altar over against the land of Canaan..." The witnesses note the report reaching the western tribes that an altar had been built by the eastern tribes. The witnesses note that Gill notes those who dwelt in Canaan heard, a proof the altar was built on their side. The witnesses note that the report spread of the altar over against the land of Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the children of Israel heard — the report; the an altar built — by the eastern tribes; the without its meaning — a bare report; the hasty conclusions — folly to answer before hearing (Prov. 18:13).

The witnesses agree the building of the altar quickly came to the ears of the western tribes — "the children of Israel heard say... have built an altar": the report, coming without the explanation of its purpose, set in motion a grave alarm; the bare news of an altar, apart from the altar of the LORD at Shiloh, seemed to threaten a rival worship; it teaches how easily a good action may be misreported and misjudged when its meaning is not known, and warns against forming hasty conclusions on partial report, "he that answereth a matter before he heareth it, it is folly and shame unto him" (Prov. 18:13), the report of the altar reaching Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse Israel heard: The witnesses note the the children of Israel heard: the report. The witnesses note the an altar built: by the eastern tribes. The witnesses note the without its meaning: a bare report. The witnesses note the hasty conclusions: folly to answer before hearing (Prov. 18:13). The expositors observe the the children of Israel heard: the report. The expositors observe the an altar built: by the eastern tribes. The expositors observe the without its meaning: a bare report. The expositors observe the hasty conclusions: folly to answer before hearing (Prov. 18:13). The believer is taught to mark the the children of Israel heard, the an altar built, the without its meaning, the hasty conclusions, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the report of the altar reached the western tribes — a good action misreported without its meaning, the danger of hasty conclusions on partial report, folly to answer a matter before hearing it (Prov. 18:13).
12:
"And when the children of Israel heard of it, the whole congregation of the children of Israel gathered themselves together at Shiloh, to go up to war against them." The witnesses note the whole congregation gathering at Shiloh to make war on the eastern tribes for the supposed apostasy. The witnesses note that Gill notes the nine tribes and a half settled in Canaan heard of the building of the altar. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the whole congregation gathering to go to war against them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the whole congregation — at Shiloh; the to go up to war — against their brethren; the zeal for pure worship — against supposed apostasy; the zeal needing knowledge — a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge (Rom. 10:2).

The witnesses agree the alarm rose to the point of war — "the whole congregation... gathered... to go up to war against them": the western tribes, fearing a defection from the LORD and a rival altar, were ready to take arms against their brethren, even to the rooting-out of the offence; their zeal, though grounded on a mistake, sprang from a holy jealousy for the pure worship of God and the unity of his altar; it teaches that the people of God should be zealous against apostasy and idolatry, yet warns that zeal must be governed by knowledge, lest it war against the innocent, "they have a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge" (Rom. 10:2), Israel gathering against the eastern tribes.

So the witnesses make the verse gathered to war: The witnesses note the the whole congregation: at Shiloh. The witnesses note the to go up to war: against their brethren. The witnesses note the zeal for pure worship: against supposed apostasy. The witnesses note the zeal needing knowledge: a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge (Rom. 10:2). The expositors observe the the whole congregation: at Shiloh. The expositors observe the to go up to war: against their brethren. The expositors observe the zeal for pure worship: against supposed apostasy. The expositors observe the zeal needing knowledge: a zeal of God, but not according to knowledge (Rom. 10:2). The believer is taught to mark the the whole congregation, the to go up to war, the zeal for pure worship, the zeal needing knowledge, and to cleave unto the LORD with all his heart. The verse thus records that the whole congregation gathered to war against the eastern tribes — a holy jealousy for pure worship, yet grounded on a mistake, zeal needing to be governed by knowledge, a zeal of God but not according to knowledge (Rom. 10:2).
13:
"And the children of Israel sent unto the children of Reuben... Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest," The witnesses note Israel sending Phinehas the priest to the eastern tribes before going to war. The witnesses note that Gill notes the children of Israel in the land of Canaan sent the embassy. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes Phinehas was well suited to the office, by the constitution of priests and judges Moses established. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israel sent — before war; the Phinehas the priest — zealous at Peor; the an embassy of inquiry — godly restraint; the inquiry before action — go and tell him his fault (Matt. 18:15).

The witnesses agree that before drawing the sword, Israel wisely "sent... Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest" to inquire: the choosing of Phinehas, who had once turned away wrath at Peor by his zeal (Num. 25:7-13), shows a man fit to deal with a matter of God's worship; the sending of an embassy before war marks a godly restraint, that the truth might be known before judgment fell; it teaches the wisdom of inquiry before action, going first to learn the fault rather than rushing to punish, "go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone" (Matt. 18:15), Phinehas sent to the eastern tribes.

So the witnesses make the verse Phinehas sent: The witnesses note the Israel sent: before war. The witnesses note the Phinehas the priest: zealous at Peor. The witnesses note the an embassy of inquiry: godly restraint. The witnesses note the inquiry before action: go and tell him his fault (Matt. 18:15). The expositors observe the Israel sent: before war. The expositors observe the Phinehas the priest: zealous at Peor. The expositors observe the an embassy of inquiry: godly restraint. The expositors observe the inquiry before action: go and tell him his fault (Matt. 18:15). The believer is taught to mark the Israel sent, the Phinehas the priest, the an embassy of inquiry, the inquiry before action, and to keep clear of all rebellion against the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel sent Phinehas the priest to inquire before going to war — godly restraint, the truth sought before judgment, going first to learn the fault, go and tell him his fault between thee and him (Matt. 18:15).
14:
"And with him ten princes, of each chief house a prince throughout all the tribes of Israel..." The witnesses note ten princes, one from each tribe, sent with Phinehas. The witnesses note that Gill notes ten princes, of each chief house a prince. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set each one a head of the house of their fathers among the thousands of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ten princes — one per tribe; the of each chief house — full authority; the with the priest — religious and civil heads; the weighty counsel — safety in many counsellors (Prov. 11:14).

The witnesses agree the embassy was weighty and representative — "ten princes, of each chief house a prince throughout all the tribes": the sending of a head from every tribe gave the mission the full authority of the whole congregation, and showed how grave the matter was esteemed; the joining of the priest with the princes set the religious and civil heads together in the cause; it teaches that matters touching the worship and unity of God's people are to be handled with full and weighty counsel, not by a few, the leaders of all gathered to deal with so great a question, "in the multitude of counsellors there is safety" (Prov. 11:14), ten princes sent with Phinehas.

So the witnesses make the verse ten princes: The witnesses note the ten princes: one per tribe. The witnesses note the of each chief house: full authority. The witnesses note the with the priest: religious and civil heads. The witnesses note the weighty counsel: safety in many counsellors (Prov. 11:14). The expositors observe the ten princes: one per tribe. The expositors observe the of each chief house: full authority. The expositors observe the with the priest: religious and civil heads. The expositors observe the weighty counsel: safety in many counsellors (Prov. 11:14). The believer is taught to mark the ten princes, the of each chief house, the with the priest, the weighty counsel, and to keep clear of all rebellion against the LORD. The verse thus records that ten princes, one from each tribe, were sent with Phinehas — the matter handled with full and weighty counsel, the leaders of all gathered, safety in the multitude of counsellors (Prov. 11:14).
15:
"And they came unto the children of Reuben, and to the children of Gad, and to the half tribe of Manasseh, unto the land of Gilead, and they spake with them, saying," The witnesses note the deputation coming to the eastern tribes in Gilead to speak with them. The witnesses note that Gill notes the deputation came to Gilead, either sending for the chiefs or coming to them. The witnesses note that they spake with them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the they came — to Gilead; the to the eastern tribes — face to face; the and spake with them — directly; the hearing before condemning — go and tell him (Matt. 18:15).

The witnesses agree the embassy did not act from afar but "came unto" the eastern tribes "and spake with them" face to face: the willingness to go and speak directly, rather than condemn at a distance, shows the right way of dealing with a brother suspected of fault; the seeking of a personal hearing before any judgment marks fairness and love; it teaches that the people of God are to deal with one another openly and directly, hearing before condemning, going to the brother in person, "if thy brother trespass against thee, go and tell him" (Matt. 18:15), the deputation coming to speak with the eastern tribes.

So the witnesses make the verse they came and spake: The witnesses note the they came: to Gilead. The witnesses note the to the eastern tribes: face to face. The witnesses note the and spake with them: directly. The witnesses note the hearing before condemning: go and tell him (Matt. 18:15). The expositors observe the they came: to Gilead. The expositors observe the to the eastern tribes: face to face. The expositors observe the and spake with them: directly. The expositors observe the hearing before condemning: go and tell him (Matt. 18:15). The believer is taught to mark the they came, the to the eastern tribes, the and spake with them, the hearing before condemning, and to keep clear of all rebellion against the LORD. The verse thus records that the deputation came to Gilead and spake with the eastern tribes face to face — dealing openly and directly, hearing before condemning, going to the brother in person (Matt. 18:15).
16:
"Thus saith the whole congregation of the LORD, What trespass is this that ye have committed against the God of Israel, to turn away this day from following the LORD, in that ye have builded you an altar, that ye might rebel this day against the LORD?" The witnesses note the charge — that by building the altar the eastern tribes had rebelled against the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they speak in the name of the congregation of the LORD, not of Israel, because it was a religious, not a civil, matter. The witnesses note that they charge them with turning away from following the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the congregation of the LORD — a religious cause; the what trespass is this — the charge; the turn away from the LORD — the alleged sin; the jealous for his honour — the zeal of thine house (Ps. 69:9).

The witnesses agree the embassy spoke in the name of "the whole congregation of the LORD," marking the cause as religious, a matter of God's honour: Gill notes they say "of the LORD," not "of Israel," for they came not to plead their own cause but God's; the charge — "that ye might rebel... against the LORD" — shows how gravely a breach in the unity of worship was regarded; it teaches that the people of God are to be jealous for his honour above all, counting any turning from him the worst of trespasses, and pleading his cause and not their own, "the zeal of thine house hath eaten me up" (Ps. 69:9), the charge brought in the name of the congregation of the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the charge of rebellion: The witnesses note the the congregation of the LORD: a religious cause. The witnesses note the what trespass is this: the charge. The witnesses note the turn away from the LORD: the alleged sin. The witnesses note the jealous for his honour: the zeal of thine house (Ps. 69:9). The expositors observe the the congregation of the LORD: a religious cause. The expositors observe the what trespass is this: the charge. The expositors observe the turn away from the LORD: the alleged sin. The expositors observe the jealous for his honour: the zeal of thine house (Ps. 69:9). The believer is taught to mark the the congregation of the LORD, the what trespass is this, the turn away from the LORD, the jealous for his honour, and to keep clear of all rebellion against the LORD. The verse thus records that the embassy charged the eastern tribes with rebelling against the LORD by the altar — pleading God's cause and not their own, jealous for his honour above all, the zeal of his house (Ps. 69:9).
17:
"Is the iniquity of Peor too little for us, from which we are not cleansed until this day, although there was a plague in the congregation of the LORD," The witnesses note the embassy recalling the sin of Peor, whose guilt was not yet wholly purged. The witnesses note that Gill notes the worshipping of Peor in the plains of Moab — was that sin so small that another must be added to it? The witnesses note that there was a plague in the congregation for it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the iniquity of Peor — the former sin; the not yet cleansed — its guilt lingering; the a plague in the congregation — the past judgment; the a warning remembered — written for our admonition (1 Cor. 10:11).

The witnesses agree the embassy pressed the memory of "the iniquity of Peor," when Israel joined to Baal-peor and a plague slew thousands (Num. 25:1-9): was that sin "too little," they ask, that a new defection should be added? the recalling of past judgment as a warning shows how sin once committed leaves a lasting fear; the embassy uses the memory to dissuade from fresh apostasy; it teaches that the remembrance of God's past judgments on sin should restrain his people from like provocations, the scars of former chastisement a warning, "now these things... are written for our admonition" (1 Cor. 10:11), the iniquity of Peor recalled.

So the witnesses make the verse the iniquity of Peor: The witnesses note the the iniquity of Peor: the former sin. The witnesses note the not yet cleansed: its guilt lingering. The witnesses note the a plague in the congregation: the past judgment. The witnesses note the a warning remembered: written for our admonition (1 Cor. 10:11). The verse thus records that the embassy recalled the iniquity of Peor as a warning against fresh apostasy — the remembrance of God's past judgments restraining his people, these things written for our admonition (1 Cor. 10:11).
18:
"But that ye must turn away this day from following the LORD? and it will be, seeing ye rebel to day against the LORD, that to morrow he will be wroth with the whole congregation of Israel." The witnesses note the warning that the sin of these tribes would bring God's wrath on the whole nation. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not content with the former transgression, but must add this revolt. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the fear that God would be wroth with the whole congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turn away from the LORD — the alleged revolt; the ye rebel to day — the charge; the wroth with the whole — corporate judgment; the all bound together — one member suffers, all suffer (1 Cor. 12:26).

The witnesses agree the embassy feared that the supposed rebellion of the eastern tribes would draw down wrath "with the whole congregation of Israel": they reasoned from the solidarity of God's people, that the sin of one part endangers the whole; the dread of corporate judgment for the offence of a few shows how the people of God are bound together in weal and woe; it teaches that no man's sin is merely his own, but may bring trouble on all, and that all have a stake in each other's faithfulness, "whether one member suffer, all the members suffer with it" (1 Cor. 12:26), the fear of wrath on the whole congregation.

So the witnesses make the verse wrath on the whole: The witnesses note the turn away from the LORD: the alleged revolt. The witnesses note the ye rebel to day: the charge. The witnesses note the wroth with the whole: corporate judgment. The witnesses note the all bound together: one member suffers, all suffer (1 Cor. 12:26). The expositors observe the turn away from the LORD: the alleged revolt. The expositors observe the ye rebel to day: the charge. The expositors observe the wroth with the whole: corporate judgment. The expositors observe the all bound together: one member suffers, all suffer (1 Cor. 12:26). The believer is taught to mark the turn away from the LORD, the ye rebel to day, the wroth with the whole, the all bound together, and to keep clear of all rebellion against the LORD. The verse thus records that the embassy feared the eastern tribes' sin would bring wrath on the whole congregation — the solidarity of God's people, no man's sin merely his own, when one member suffers all suffer with it (1 Cor. 12:26).
19:
"Notwithstanding, if the land of your possession be unclean, then pass ye over unto the land of the possession of the LORD... and take possession among us: but rebel not against the LORD, nor rebel against us, in building you an altar beside the altar of the LORD our God." The witnesses note the embassy's generous offer — to share their own land rather than have the eastern tribes build a rival altar. The witnesses note that Gill notes if their land was judged unclean, having no altar to expiate sin, they were invited over. The witnesses note that they were not to build an altar beside the altar of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if the land be unclean — their concern; the take possession among us — sharing their land; the rebel not — keep the unity; the peace above possessions — follow the things that make for peace (Rom. 14:19).

The witnesses agree the embassy tempered their rebuke with a remarkable offer of love — "if the land of your possession be unclean... take possession among us": rather than see their brethren build a rival altar, they would share their own inheritance with them; the readiness to give up of their own portion to keep the unity of worship shows a true and self-denying love; it teaches that the people of God should prize the unity of his worship above their own possessions, willing to make sacrifices to keep their brethren near the altar of the LORD, "let us follow after the things which make for peace" (Rom. 14:19), the offer to share their land.

So the witnesses make the verse take possession among us: The witnesses note the if the land be unclean: their concern. The witnesses note the take possession among us: sharing their land. The witnesses note the rebel not: keep the unity. The witnesses note the peace above possessions: follow the things that make for peace (Rom. 14:19). The verse thus records that the embassy offered to share their own land rather than have a rival altar built — a self-denying love prizing the unity of worship above possessions, following after the things that make for peace (Rom. 14:19).
20:
"Did not Achan the son of Zerah commit a trespass in the accursed thing, and wrath fell on all the congregation of Israel? and that man perished not alone in his iniquity." The witnesses note the embassy citing Achan, whose one sin brought wrath on all Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes Achan descended from Zerah the son of Judah. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Achan's trespass in the accursed thing, and wrath falling on all the congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Achan's trespass — in the accursed thing; the wrath on all — the congregation; the perished not alone — others drawn in; the sin's reach — a little leaven leavens the lump (1 Cor. 5:6).

The witnesses agree the embassy clinched their warning with the case of Achan — "did not Achan... commit a trespass... and wrath fell on all the congregation?": the one man's secret sin had brought defeat and death on the whole nation, and "that man perished not alone in his iniquity"; the recalling of Achan presses home that the sin of a part endangers all, and that the guilty draw others into their ruin; it teaches the dreadful reach of sin in the body of God's people, one man's transgression troubling many, a solemn motive to keep the whole congregation pure, "a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump" (1 Cor. 5:6), Achan's sin recalled as a warning.

So the witnesses make the verse Achan recalled: The witnesses note the Achan's trespass: in the accursed thing. The witnesses note the wrath on all: the congregation. The witnesses note the perished not alone: others drawn in. The witnesses note the sin's reach: a little leaven leavens the lump (1 Cor. 5:6). 21:
"Then the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh answered, and said unto the heads of the thousands of Israel," The witnesses note the eastern tribes answering the embassy through a chosen spokesman. The witnesses note that Gill notes they answered by some person appointed to deliver the answer in their name. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the eastern tribes answering the heads of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the eastern tribes answered — through a spokesman; the to the heads of Israel — reverently; the no resentment — a peaceable spirit; the meekness when suspected — a soft answer turns away wrath (Prov. 15:1).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes did not resent the charge but meekly "answered... unto the heads of the thousands of Israel": rather than take offence at the suspicion, they gave a calm and reverent reply; the readiness to answer and clear themselves, without anger at being questioned, shows a humble and peaceable spirit; it teaches that when wrongly suspected, the people of God should answer with meekness and seek to clear the truth, not flaring into resentment, "a soft answer turneth away wrath" (Prov. 15:1), the eastern tribes answering the heads of Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse they answered: The witnesses note the the eastern tribes answered: through a spokesman. The witnesses note the to the heads of Israel: reverently. The witnesses note the no resentment: a peaceable spirit. The witnesses note the meekness when suspected: a soft answer turns away wrath (Prov. 15:1). The expositors observe the the eastern tribes answered: through a spokesman. The expositors observe the to the heads of Israel: reverently. The expositors observe the no resentment: a peaceable spirit. The expositors observe the meekness when suspected: a soft answer turns away wrath (Prov. 15:1). The verse thus records that the eastern tribes answered the embassy meekly through a spokesman — answering wrongful suspicion without resentment, with a humble and peaceable spirit, a soft answer turning away wrath (Prov. 15:1).
22:
"The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth, and Israel he shall know; if it be in rebellion, or if in transgression against the LORD, (save us not this day,)" The witnesses note the eastern tribes' solemn appeal to the all-knowing God of gods. The witnesses note that Gill notes they set out asserting their firm belief that Jehovah, the God of Israel, is the supreme God, God over all. The witnesses note that they appealed to him who knoweth, saying, if in rebellion, save us not this day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD God of gods — the supreme God; the he knoweth — the heart-searcher; the if in rebellion — their solemn oath; the a clear conscience — thou only knowest the hearts of men (1 Kgs 8:39).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes opened their defence with a most solemn and reverent appeal — "The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth": the threefold naming of God and the doubled confession assert their faith in the supreme LORD and call him to witness their hearts; the willingness to be unsaved if guilty — "save us not this day" — shows the boldness of a clear conscience that fears no searching; it teaches that the people of God may appeal to him who knows all hearts when wrongly charged, resting their cause in his omniscience, "thou, even thou only, knowest the hearts of all the children of men" (1 Kgs 8:39), the eastern tribes appealing to the God who knoweth.

So the witnesses make the verse he knoweth: The witnesses note the the LORD God of gods: the supreme God. The witnesses note the he knoweth: the heart-searcher. The witnesses note the if in rebellion: their solemn oath. The witnesses note the a clear conscience: thou only knowest the hearts of men (1 Kgs 8:39). The expositors observe the the LORD God of gods: the supreme God. The expositors observe the he knoweth: the heart-searcher. The expositors observe the if in rebellion: their solemn oath. The expositors observe the a clear conscience: thou only knowest the hearts of men (1 Kgs 8:39). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD God of gods, the he knoweth, the if in rebellion, the a clear conscience, and to keep clear of all rebellion against the LORD. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes appealed to the LORD God of gods, who knoweth all hearts — the boldness of a clear conscience resting its cause in his omniscience, thou only knowest the hearts of all men (1 Kgs 8:39).
23:
"That we have built us an altar to turn from following the LORD, or if to offer thereon burnt offering or meat offering, or if to offer peace offerings thereon, let the LORD himself require it;" The witnesses note the eastern tribes invoking God's judgment on themselves if the altar were for rival sacrifice. The witnesses note that Gill notes they desire, as they deserve, to be cut off and not saved, if their view in building was to revolt from the pure worship of God. The witnesses note that they say, let the LORD himself require it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the built an altar to turn away — the charge denied; the to offer sacrifice — if for rival worship; the let the LORD require it — divine judgment invoked; the upright before God — judge me, for I have walked in integrity (Ps. 26:1).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes called down God's own judgment upon themselves should the altar be for rival worship — "if to offer thereon burnt offering... let the LORD himself require it": they would not shrink from divine vengeance if guilty, so sure were they of their innocence; the readiness to be judged by God himself shows the confidence of an upright heart and the abhorrence of the very sin charged; it teaches that the people of God, conscious of their integrity, may submit their cause to his righteous judgment, and that a clear conscience dreads not his searching, "judge me, O LORD; for I have walked in mine integrity" (Ps. 26:1), the eastern tribes submitting to God's requital if guilty.

So the witnesses make the verse let the LORD require it: The witnesses note the built an altar to turn away: the charge denied. The witnesses note the to offer sacrifice: if for rival worship. The witnesses note the let the LORD require it: divine judgment invoked. The witnesses note the upright before God: judge me, for I have walked in integrity (Ps. 26:1). The verse thus records that the eastern tribes invoked God's own judgment if the altar were for rival sacrifice — the confidence of an upright heart submitting to his righteous judgment, judge me, O LORD, for I have walked in mine integrity (Ps. 26:1).
24:
"And if we have not rather done it for fear of this thing, saying, In time to come your children might speak unto our children, saying, What have ye to do with the LORD God of Israel?" The witnesses note the eastern tribes' true reason — fear that their children might one day be told they had no part in the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were so far from turning to idolatry that it was to guard against everything of the kind for the future. The witnesses note that they feared their children might be cut off from the LORD God of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the for fear of this thing — the true motive; the in time to come — their children's faith; the what have ye to do — lest shut out; the care for those after — the generation to come might know (Ps. 78:6).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes now reveal their true and pious motive — "for fear of this thing," lest "in time to come your children might speak unto our children... What have ye to do with the LORD God of Israel?": their concern was not for the present but for the faith of generations to come, that the Jordan should not be made a wall to shut their children out from the worship of the LORD; the forethought for the religion of their descendants shows a godly care; it teaches that the people of God are to look to the faith of those who come after, taking pains that their children be not cut off from the LORD, "that the generation to come might know them... who should arise and declare them to their children" (Ps. 78:6), the altar built for fear for their children.

So the witnesses make the verse fear for their children: The witnesses note the for fear of this thing: the true motive. The witnesses note the in time to come: their children's faith. The witnesses note the what have ye to do: lest shut out. The witnesses note the care for those after: the generation to come might know (Ps. 78:6). The expositors observe the for fear of this thing: the true motive. The expositors observe the in time to come: their children's faith. The expositors observe the what have ye to do: lest shut out. The expositors observe the care for those after: the generation to come might know (Ps. 78:6). The believer is taught to mark the for fear of this thing, the in time to come, the what have ye to do, the care for those after, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes built the altar for fear their children might be told they had no part in the LORD — a godly care for the faith of generations to come, that the generation to come might know and declare them to their children (Ps. 78:6).
25:
"For the LORD hath made Jordan a border between us and you, ye children of Reuben and children of Gad; ye have no part in the LORD: so shall your children make our children cease from fearing the LORD." The witnesses note the fear that Jordan might be made a wall of division, their children cut off from fearing the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they feared it would be represented in time to come that Jordan separated them as a distinct people. The witnesses note that so their children might be made to cease from fearing the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jordan a border — a natural division; the ye have no part — the feared exclusion; the cease from fearing — their children driven off; the all one in him — neither Jew nor Greek, all one in Christ (Gal. 3:28).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes dreaded that the Jordan, a mere natural border, might in after-times be turned into a spiritual wall — "ye have no part in the LORD" — so that their children should "cease from fearing the LORD": they feared a schism not of doctrine but of unbrotherly exclusion, which might drive their offspring from the faith; the dread of being cut off from the people of God and his worship shows how they prized their part in the LORD; it teaches that the unity of God's people is not to be broken by outward divisions, and that none should be thrust from the common faith for place or circumstance, "there is neither Jew nor Greek... ye are all one in Christ Jesus" (Gal. 3:28), the fear of being shut out from the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse no part in the LORD: The witnesses note the Jordan a border: a natural division. The witnesses note the ye have no part: the feared exclusion. The witnesses note the cease from fearing: their children driven off. The witnesses note the all one in him: neither Jew nor Greek, all one in Christ (Gal. 3:28). The expositors observe the Jordan a border: a natural division. The expositors observe the ye have no part: the feared exclusion. The expositors observe the cease from fearing: their children driven off. The expositors observe the all one in him: neither Jew nor Greek, all one in Christ (Gal. 3:28). The believer is taught to mark the Jordan a border, the ye have no part, the cease from fearing, the all one in him, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that they feared Jordan might be made a wall, their children told they had no part in the LORD — the unity of God's people not to be broken by outward divisions, none thrust from the faith, all one in Christ Jesus (Gal. 3:28).
26:
"Therefore we said, Let us now prepare to build us an altar, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice:" The witnesses note the eastern tribes' purpose — an altar not for sacrifice, but for another end. The witnesses note that Gill notes they said this one to another, to prevent the apostasy of their children and their going into idolatry. The witnesses note that Clarke notes an altar not for burnt-offering nor sacrifice, which would have been sin. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let us build an altar — their purpose; the not for burnt offering — nor sacrifice; the reverence for God's order — the one-altar command; the worship by his rule — not the commandments of men (Matt. 15:9).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes are careful to declare the altar was "not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice": Clarke notes that to offer sacrifice there would itself have been the very sin charged, a breach of the one-altar command; their pains to make plain the altar's true purpose show their reverence for God's appointed order of worship; it teaches that the people of God must guard the purity of his worship and avoid even the appearance of breaking his commands, doing all to his honour and according to his rule, "in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men" (Matt. 15:9) — the altar built not for sacrifice but for witness.

So the witnesses make the verse not for sacrifice: The witnesses note the let us build an altar: their purpose. The witnesses note the not for burnt offering: nor sacrifice. The witnesses note the reverence for God's order: the one-altar command. The witnesses note the worship by his rule: not the commandments of men (Matt. 15:9). The expositors observe the let us build an altar: their purpose. The expositors observe the not for burnt offering: nor sacrifice. The expositors observe the reverence for God's order: the one-altar command. The expositors observe the worship by his rule: not the commandments of men (Matt. 15:9). The believer is taught to mark the let us build an altar, the not for burnt offering, the reverence for God's order, the worship by his rule, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes built the altar "not for sacrifice" — reverencing God's appointed order of worship, avoiding even the appearance of breaking his command, worshipping by his rule and not man's (Matt. 15:9).
27:
"But that it may be a witness between us and you, and our generations after us, that we might do the service of the LORD before him... that your children may not say to our children in time to come, Ye have no part in the LORD." The witnesses note the altar's true purpose — a witness that the eastern tribes share in the LORD and his service. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was to witness that they are one people, worship one God, and serve at one altar, of which this was a resemblance. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the altar as a witness for the generations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a witness — between us and you; the our generations after — the bond kept; the the service of the LORD — shared worship; the a memorial of unity — a memorial throughout your generations (Exod. 12:14).

The witnesses agree the altar's whole design was to be "a witness between us and you, and our generations after us": a standing memorial that the eastern tribes shared in the LORD, his worship, and his altar, "a resemblance" of the true altar at Shiloh; the building of a witness to keep alive the bond of common faith shows the worth of visible tokens of unity among God's people; it teaches that the people of God do well to set up memorials of their shared faith and fellowship, that the bond be not forgotten by those who come after, "this shall be a memorial... throughout your generations" (Exod. 12:14), the altar a witness of their part in the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse a witness: The witnesses note the a witness: between us and you. The witnesses note the our generations after: the bond kept. The witnesses note the the service of the LORD: shared worship. The witnesses note the a memorial of unity: a memorial throughout your generations (Exod. 12:14). The expositors observe the a witness: between us and you. The expositors observe the our generations after: the bond kept. The expositors observe the the service of the LORD: shared worship. The expositors observe the a memorial of unity: a memorial throughout your generations (Exod. 12:14). The believer is taught to mark the a witness, the our generations after, the the service of the LORD, the a memorial of unity, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the altar was a witness that the eastern tribes shared in the LORD and his service — a memorial of common faith, that the bond be not forgotten by those after, a memorial throughout your generations (Exod. 12:14).
28:
"Therefore said we, that it shall be, when they should so say to us or to our generations in time to come, that we may say again, Behold the pattern of the altar of the LORD, which our fathers made, not for burnt offerings, nor for sacrifices; but it is a witness between us and you." The witnesses note the altar made a ready answer for the children, a pattern of the LORD's altar. The witnesses note that Gill notes they said this to prevent such usage of their children, and that they might have a ready answer. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the words suggest the altar was a pattern of the altar of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a ready answer — for the children; the the pattern of the altar — a resemblance; the not for sacrifice — a witness; the give an answer — a reason of the hope in you (1 Pet. 3:15).

The witnesses agree the altar was meant to furnish a "ready answer" for their children — "Behold the pattern of the altar of the LORD... it is a witness": made on the model of the true altar, it would let their descendants point to it and prove their share in the worship of the LORD; the providing of an answer for the generations to come shows a wise forethought for the faith of children; it teaches that the people of God should be ready to give an account of their faith and to leave clear testimony to those after them, "be ready always to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you" (1 Pet. 3:15), the altar a pattern and a witness for the children.

So the witnesses make the verse a ready answer: The witnesses note the a ready answer: for the children. The witnesses note the the pattern of the altar: a resemblance. The witnesses note the not for sacrifice: a witness. The witnesses note the give an answer: a reason of the hope in you (1 Pet. 3:15). The expositors observe the a ready answer: for the children. The expositors observe the the pattern of the altar: a resemblance. The expositors observe the not for sacrifice: a witness. The expositors observe the give an answer: a reason of the hope in you (1 Pet. 3:15). The believer is taught to mark the a ready answer, the the pattern of the altar, the not for sacrifice, the give an answer, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the altar furnished a ready answer for the children — a pattern of the LORD's altar and a witness, a wise forethought for the faith of those after, ready to give an answer for the hope that is in us (1 Pet. 3:15).
29:
"God forbid that we should rebel against the LORD, and turn this day from following the LORD, to build an altar for burnt offerings... beside the altar of the LORD our God that is before his tabernacle." The witnesses note the eastern tribes' abhorrence of the very rebellion charged against them. The witnesses note that Gill notes God ordered one altar, to which all sacrifices were to be brought from every part of Israel. The witnesses note that they would not build an altar beside the altar of the LORD before his tabernacle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the God forbid — their abhorrence; the rebel against the LORD — the charge denied; the beside the altar of the LORD — no rival; the the one altar — one offering perfecting for ever (Heb. 10:14).

The witnesses agree the eastern tribes close their defence with a strong disavowal — "God forbid that we should rebel against the LORD... to build an altar... beside the altar of the LORD": they abhorred the thought of setting up a rival to the one altar God had appointed at his tabernacle; the vehemence of their refusal shows their reverence for the unity and purity of God's worship; it teaches that the people of God should hold sacred his appointed way of worship and shrink from any rival or addition, owning the one altar and the one sacrifice, a figure of the "one offering" by which Christ "hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified" (Heb. 10:14), the eastern tribes refusing any altar beside the LORD's.

So the witnesses make the verse God forbid: The witnesses note the God forbid: their abhorrence. The witnesses note the rebel against the LORD: the charge denied. The witnesses note the beside the altar of the LORD: no rival. The witnesses note the the one altar: one offering perfecting for ever (Heb. 10:14). The expositors observe the God forbid: their abhorrence. The expositors observe the rebel against the LORD: the charge denied. The expositors observe the beside the altar of the LORD: no rival. The expositors observe the the one altar: one offering perfecting for ever (Heb. 10:14). The believer is taught to mark the God forbid, the rebel against the LORD, the beside the altar of the LORD, the the one altar, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the eastern tribes abhorred the thought of a rival altar — reverencing the unity and purity of God's worship, owning the one altar, a figure of the one offering by which Christ has perfected for ever the sanctified (Heb. 10:14).
30:
"And when Phinehas the priest, and the princes of the congregation and heads of the thousands of Israel... heard the words... it pleased them." The witnesses note Phinehas and the princes well satisfied with the eastern tribes' explanation. The witnesses note that Gill notes Phinehas was the son of Eleazar the high priest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Phinehas and the princes hearing the words, and it pleasing them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Phinehas and the princes — heard; the the words — the true account; the it pleased them — satisfied; the glad of the truth — charity rejoiceth in the truth (1 Cor. 13:6).

The witnesses agree the embassy, on hearing the true account, was satisfied — "it pleased them": the readiness of Phinehas and the princes to be content once the matter was explained shows the fairness of men who sought the truth and not a quarrel; their willingness to lay down their suspicion upon good evidence marks a peaceable and just spirit; it teaches that the people of God should be ready to be satisfied when a brother clears himself, putting away suspicion gladly and rejoicing in his innocence, "charity... rejoiceth not in iniquity, but rejoiceth in the truth" (1 Cor. 13:6), the explanation pleasing Phinehas and the princes.

So the witnesses make the verse it pleased them: The witnesses note the Phinehas and the princes: heard. The witnesses note the the words: the true account. The witnesses note the it pleased them: satisfied. The witnesses note the glad of the truth: charity rejoiceth in the truth (1 Cor. 13:6). The expositors observe the Phinehas and the princes: heard. The expositors observe the the words: the true account. The expositors observe the it pleased them: satisfied. The expositors observe the glad of the truth: charity rejoiceth in the truth (1 Cor. 13:6). The believer is taught to mark the Phinehas and the princes, the the words, the it pleased them, the glad of the truth, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that Phinehas and the princes were well satisfied with the explanation — fair men seeking truth not a quarrel, ready to lay down suspicion, charity rejoicing in the truth (1 Cor. 13:6).
31:
"And Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest said unto the children of Reuben... This day we perceive that the LORD is among us, because ye have not committed this trespass against the LORD..." The witnesses note Phinehas declaring that the LORD is among them, the feared trespass not committed. The witnesses note that Gill notes Phinehas spoke as the mouth of the deputation, in their name. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Phinehas declaring the LORD among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Phinehas said — as the mouth; the the LORD is among us — his presence; the ye have not trespassed — the faithfulness; the he abides with the faithful — we will make our abode with him (John 14:23).

The witnesses agree Phinehas read the happy outcome as a token of God's presence — "this day we perceive that the LORD is among us, because ye have not committed this trespass": the faithfulness of the eastern tribes, and the peaceful clearing of the matter, were to him a sign that God dwelt with his people; the preserving of his people from sin and from needless war was the proof of his gracious presence; it teaches that the LORD is known to be among his people when they are kept faithful and at peace, the absence of trespass and the presence of unity tokens of his nearness, "if a man love me... we will come unto him, and make our abode with him" (John 14:23), Phinehas perceiving the LORD among them.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD among us: The witnesses note the Phinehas said: as the mouth. The witnesses note the the LORD is among us: his presence. The witnesses note the ye have not trespassed: the faithfulness. The witnesses note the he abides with the faithful: we will make our abode with him (John 14:23). The expositors observe the Phinehas said: as the mouth. The expositors observe the the LORD is among us: his presence. The expositors observe the ye have not trespassed: the faithfulness. The expositors observe the he abides with the faithful: we will make our abode with him (John 14:23). The believer is taught to mark the Phinehas said, the the LORD is among us, the ye have not trespassed, the he abides with the faithful, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that Phinehas perceived the LORD among them, the trespass not committed — God known to be among his people when they are kept faithful and at peace, abiding with those who love him (John 14:23).
32:
"And Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest, and the princes, returned from the children of Reuben... unto the land of Canaan, to the children of Israel, and brought them word again." The witnesses note the embassy returning to Canaan and reporting the good outcome. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were fully satisfied with the relation given, and took their leave of the assembly. The witnesses note that they brought word again to the children of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the embassy returned — to Canaan; the to the children of Israel — the waiting congregation; the brought word again — the true report; the peacemakers — blessed are the peacemakers (Matt. 5:9).

The witnesses agree the embassy, satisfied, "returned... and brought them word again": the faithful carrying-back of the true account to the waiting congregation completed their errand of peace; the diligence to report the truth and quiet the alarm shows the care of good men to undo a misunderstanding fully; it teaches that those who deal between brethren are to bring back a true and peace-making report, that the whole body may be set at rest, the messengers of peace doing their work to the end, "blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God" (Matt. 5:9), the embassy bringing word again to Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse brought word again: The witnesses note the the embassy returned: to Canaan. The witnesses note the to the children of Israel: the waiting congregation. The witnesses note the brought word again: the true report. The witnesses note the peacemakers: blessed are the peacemakers (Matt. 5:9). The expositors observe the the embassy returned: to Canaan. The expositors observe the to the children of Israel: the waiting congregation. The expositors observe the brought word again: the true report. The expositors observe the peacemakers: blessed are the peacemakers (Matt. 5:9). The believer is taught to mark the the embassy returned, the to the children of Israel, the brought word again, the peacemakers, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the embassy returned and brought word again to Israel — the faithful carrying-back of the peace-making report, the messengers doing their work to the end, blessed are the peacemakers (Matt. 5:9).
33:
"And the thing pleased the children of Israel; and the children of Israel blessed God, and did not intend to go up against them in battle, to destroy the land wherein the children of Reuben and Gad dwelt." The witnesses note Israel pleased and blessing God, the threatened war laid aside. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were quite satisfied and delighted with what their brethren had done. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the thing pleasing Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the thing pleased Israel — they were glad; the blessed God — for peace; the no war — the threat laid aside; the brethren in unity — how good for brethren to dwell in unity (Ps. 133:1).

The witnesses agree the whole congregation was glad and "blessed God" that the matter ended in peace — the threatened war "did not" come to pass: their first thought, on hearing the truth, was to give thanks to God for preserving them from shedding their brethren's blood; the turning of alarm into praise shows the right end of every controversy among God's people — peace and thanksgiving; it teaches that the people of God should bless him for peace preserved and quarrels averted, rejoicing when strife is turned to concord, "behold, how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity!" (Ps. 133:1), Israel blessing God and laying down the war.

So the witnesses make the verse blessed God: The witnesses note the the thing pleased Israel: they were glad. The witnesses note the blessed God: for peace. The witnesses note the no war: the threat laid aside. The witnesses note the brethren in unity: how good for brethren to dwell in unity (Ps. 133:1). The expositors observe the the thing pleased Israel: they were glad. The expositors observe the blessed God: for peace. The expositors observe the no war: the threat laid aside. The expositors observe the brethren in unity: how good for brethren to dwell in unity (Ps. 133:1). The believer is taught to mark the the thing pleased Israel, the blessed God, the no war, the brethren in unity, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that Israel was pleased and blessed God, the war laid aside — the right end of every controversy peace and thanksgiving, blessing God for strife turned to concord, how good for brethren to dwell together in unity (Ps. 133:1).
34:
"And the children of Reuben and the children of Gad called the altar Ed: for it shall be a witness between us that the LORD is God." The witnesses note the altar named Ed, "a witness," that the LORD is God. The witnesses note that Gill notes Ed signifies "a witness"; they put this word upon it, that its intention might be known in time to come. The witnesses note that it shall be a witness that the LORD is God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the called the altar Ed — a witness; the the name graven — its purpose sealed; the between us — one God, one faith; the the LORD is God — the LORD, he is God (1 Kgs 18:39).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes with the altar given a name that sealed its purpose — "Ed: for it shall be a witness between us that the LORD is God": the name graven upon it would tell all generations that the eastern and western tribes owned one God and one faith; the altar of misunderstanding became a lasting monument of unity and of the truth that "the LORD is God"; it teaches that the people of God leave witnesses to his sole deity and to their common faith, that those after them may know whom they served, the altar a standing confession, "the LORD, he is God; the LORD, he is God" (1 Kgs 18:39), the altar named Ed, a witness that the LORD is God.

So the witnesses make the verse the altar named Ed: The witnesses note the called the altar Ed: a witness. The witnesses note the the name graven: its purpose sealed. The witnesses note the between us: one God, one faith. The witnesses note the the LORD is God: the LORD, he is God (1 Kgs 18:39). The expositors observe the called the altar Ed: a witness. The expositors observe the the name graven: its purpose sealed. The expositors observe the between us: one God, one faith. The expositors observe the the LORD is God: the LORD, he is God (1 Kgs 18:39). The believer is taught to mark the called the altar Ed, the the name graven, the between us, the the LORD is God, and to witness that the LORD is God. The verse thus records that the altar was named Ed, "a witness that the LORD is God" — the altar of misunderstanding become a monument of unity and confession, that those after might know whom they served, the LORD, he is God (1 Kgs 18:39).
